retrieved_docs.title: Histology, White Blood Cell -- Clinical Significance -- Labs
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, the complete blood count (CBC) test measures leukocytes. A CBC is frequently ordered to provide insight into disease processes and includes measurements of the leukocyte red blood cell and platelet totals (see Illustration. Illustration of Red Blood Cell, Platelet, and White Blood Cell). Often associated with the CBC is a differential, which refers to the relative amounts of white blood cell types (ie, neutrophil, lymphocyte, eosinophil, etc) as a percentage of the total number of WBCs. Of note, if a subtype of white blood cells seems to be elevated based on the differential, the actual value of the type of white blood cells should be calculated by multiplying the percentage listed on the differential by the total number of white blood cells. The normal range of white blood cell values is 4,000 to 11,000/µL. Anything below this range is leukopenia and anything above this range qualifies as leukocytosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Issues of Concern -- White blood cell count
retrieved_docs.content: Identification of a WBC abnormality is followed by an assessment of the white cell differential to identify which white blood cell types are affected. The differential may be reported as a percentage of the total WBC or as an absolute count for each mature white cell type. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Issues of Concern -- White blood cell count
retrieved_docs.content: WBC, or the absolute WBC, is the number of white blood cells present per blood microliter (μL). The normal reference interval in adults is usually 4500 to 11000 cells/μL. Leukopenia is defined as a low WBC and may be due to reduced production, increased utilization, or increased destruction of white blood cells. A spurious leukopenia may occur due to white blood cell clumping. An elevated white blood cell count is called leukocytosis and may be caused by an inflammatory stressor or myeloproliferative pathology. A spurious leucocytosis may occur in the setting of: Reticulocytosis, where nucleated red blood cells are counted as white blood cells Platelet clumping, where aggregate platelets are counted as white blood cells Fibrin clumps Cryoproteinemia

retrieved_docs.title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Diagnostic Tests -- Complete Blood Count (CBC)
retrieved_docs.content: Neutrophils (normal value: 1.8 to 7.8 x 103/mm^3) Lymphocytes (normal value: 0.7 to 4.5 x 103/mm^3) Monocytes (normal value: 0.1 to 1.0 x 103/mm^3) Eosinophils (normal value: 0 to 0.4 x103/mm^3) Basophils (normal value: 0 to 0.2 x 103/mm^3) Platelet Count: The number of platelets per volume of blood (normal value: 150 to 400 x 109/mm^3) Reticulocytes: The number of immature RBCs in circulating blood (normal value: 1% of total RBC count)

retrieved_docs.title: Leukocytosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians evaluate the leukocyte count by collecting a peripheral blood smear through a routine blood draw. The cell count ranges vary depending on age and race. In general, leukocyte counts are significantly higher in infants than in adults. [20] Through adolescence, lymphocytes predominate the peripheral smear. By adulthood, neutrophils are the predominant cell line.

retrieved_docs.title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The complete blood count (CBC) is one of the most commonly performed laboratory investigations in medicine and provides information regarding circulating blood cell size and quantity. [1] The standard CBC includes a measured red cell count (RBC), hemoglobin (Hb), and hematocrit (Hct), calculated red cell indices, a platelet count, and a white cell count (WBC). Most analyzers now also automatically generate a leucocyte differential.

retrieved_docs.title: Leukocytosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The precise WBC count can differ across laboratories due to variations in the upper limit of normal and the type of hematology analyzer employed. Hematology analyzers analyze whole blood samples for a CBC and differential, encompassing the RBC count, WBC count, platelet count, hemoglobin, RBC indices, and WBC differentials. These analyzers utilize cytochemistry and fluorescence techniques to distinguish between various WBC types and categorize them as low or high using preset algorithms.

retrieved_docs.title: CD4 Count -- Normal and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: The normal CD4 count range is between 500 and 1400 cells/microliters., taking into consideration laboratory variations.

retrieved_docs.title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: This reference describes each component of the CBC and discusses the interpretation of results. Though commonly utilized reference intervals are provided, all are lab-specific in practice. The local laboratory guidelines apply when interpreting a CBC.

retrieved_docs.title: Neutrophilia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: An absolute neutrophil count (ANC), defined as the percent of neutrophils in the bloodstream and adults, typically ranges between 2500 to 7000 neutrophils/microL. An increase in the WBC count of more than 11,000 cells/microL is defined as leukocytosis. Neutrophilia is the most common type of leukocytosis. It is defined as an increase in the absolute neutrophil count of approximately more than 7700 neutrophils/microL (11,000 cells/microL x 70 percent), i.e., two standard deviations above the mean.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Diagnostic Tests -- Complete Blood Count (CBC)
retrieved_docs.content: Common terms in CBC and their normal values are as follows Red Blood Cell (RBC) Count: The number of RBCs per volume of blood (normal value: 4.2 to 6.9 x 109/mm3 Hemoglobin (Hb): Amount of oxygen-carrying capacity of blood (normal value: 130 to 180 g/L in males, 120 to 160 g/L in females) Hematocrit (Hct): Percentage of whole blood occupied by packed RBCs (normal value: 45% to 62% in males, 37% to 48% in females) Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV): The measure of RBC size (normal value: 80 to 100 micromillimeter) Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH): Amount of oxygen-carrying hemoglobin inside RBCs (normal value: 27 to 32 pg/cell) Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC): Average concentration of Hb inside RBCs (normal value: 32% to 36%) White Blood Cell (WBC) Count: The number of WBCs per volume of blood (normal value: 4.3 to 11 x 109/mm3) WBC differential:

retrieved_docs.title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Issues of Concern -- Lymphocyte count
retrieved_docs.content: The lymphocyte count is the number of lymphocytes per microliter of blood and is expressed as a percentage of the WBC or as an absolute number. The normal reference interval in adults is usually 20% to 40% of the absolute WBC or 1000 to 4000 cells/µL. Lymphocytosis, which refers to an increase in the lymphocyte count, may be due to viral infection, pertussis, toxoplasmosis, tuberculosis, brucellosis, hyposplenism, or lymphoproliferative disease. Lymphocytopenia, which refers to a decrease in the lymphocyte count, may be due to viral infection, bacterial infection, autoimmune disease (such as systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, Sjogren syndrome, and inflammatory bowel disease), malignancy, myeloproliferative disease, lymphoproliferative disease, primary immunodeficiency diseases or medications. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Leukocytosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Black African, Middle Eastern, and West Indian descent also influence the WBC count and differential. Patients of these backgrounds can have reduced WBC counts and lower absolute neutrophil counts (ANCs) compared to patients of Hispanic and European descent. [21] Known as benign ethnic neutropenia, clinicians may identify this condition in a patient of African descent with no infectious concerns on history and physical examination with chronically low neutrophils on CBC. Benign ethnic neutropenia is an important consideration when evaluating a patient of African descent, as their presentation of leukocytosis may be within the normal laboratory reference range. However, the value must be elevated compared to their prior CBCs.

retrieved_docs.title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Issues of Concern -- Red blood cell count
retrieved_docs.content: RBC is the amount of red cells present per unit volume of blood and is expressed in cells per microliter (cells/μL). The normal reference interval is usually 4.6 to 6.2 million cells/μL in adult men and 4.2 to 5.4 million cells/μL in adult women. Unlike Hb, RBC does not accurately measure the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity and is not directly used to diagnose anemia. However, red cell indices are important in evaluating anemia. The RBC may be spuriously low in the setting of red cell agglutination or a sample diluted with intravenous fluid infusion. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Leukocytosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Leukocytosis denotes an age-appropriate rise in the white blood cell (WBC) count. Typically, a WBC count surpassing 11,000 cells/µL in adults is deemed leukocytosis, and a WBC count >100,000 cells/µL is termed hyperleukocytosis. [1] However, the threshold varies with age and pregnancy; for instance, 30,000 cells/µL in adults would be abnormal but within the normal range for a newborn. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2674
retrieved_docs.content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض, and a فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي is also clinically associated with قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض. Consequently, leukopenia serves as a common clinical link between this symptom complex and HIV infection, indicating that patients presenting with low white‑blood‑cell counts should be evaluated for underlying HIV disease and related symptomatology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1431
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concept “deficiency and other anemia” is mapped to the Arabic term “قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض,” which denotes leukopenia—a condition characterized by a reduced white‑blood‑cell count. This mapping indicates that deficiencies leading to anemia are described using the term leukopenia in Arabic medical terminology, highlighting the relationship between anemia‑related deficiencies and low white‑blood‑cell levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2781
retrieved_docs.content: Diseases of white blood cells may be treated with meropenem anhydrous, indicating that this antibiotic can be used therapeutically to manage infections or conditions involving abnormal or compromised white blood cell function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2701
retrieved_docs.content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض (low white‑blood‑cell count). In addition, عدم تحمل بروتين اللايسين (lysine protein intolerance) has قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض as a manifestation. Consequently, leukopenia serves as a common clinical feature linking the SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX and lysine protein intolerance, underscoring its importance in diagnostic assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
retrieved_docs.content: Karmustin and Merkaptopurin are both contraindicated in the presence of diseases of white blood cells, indicating that these medications should be avoided in patients who have disorders affecting white blood cell function or count.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2298
retrieved_docs.content: Ceftazidime and meropenem anhydrous are antimicrobial agents that may be used to treat diseases of white blood cells. Both drugs are employed in therapeutic strategies aimed at managing infections or complications affecting white blood cell function, reflecting their role in addressing bacterial involvement in hematologic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2579
retrieved_docs.content: A positive HIV test result (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي) is clinically associated with leukopenia (قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض). Leukopenia is also classified as a member of the drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms syndrome (متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ)). This connection indicates that a low white‑blood‑cell count can arise both from HIV infection and as a component of severe drug‑induced hypersensitivity reactions, emphasizing the need for careful monitoring of white‑blood‑cell levels in patients with HIV or those exposed to medications that may trigger this syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2723
retrieved_docs.content: Ciprofloxacin, ceftazidim, and meropenem anhydrous are antimicrobial agents that may be employed in the treatment of diseases affecting white blood cells. These antibiotics are considered potential therapeutic options for managing infections or disorders that involve white blood cell pathology, highlighting their relevance in clinical decisions aimed at controlling such conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2721
retrieved_docs.content: Amphotericin B is identified as a therapeutic agent that may be used to treat diseases of white blood cells, indicating its potential role in managing conditions that affect the immune cell population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1166
retrieved_docs.content: Stomach cancer, referred to as “cancer of stomach” and also named سرطان المعدة in Arabic, is clinically associated with a low white‑blood‑cell count (قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض). This leukopenia is further linked to a broader SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX and is commonly observed in patients who have a positive HIV test (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي). Consequently, leukopenia serves as a shared clinical finding that connects gastrointestinal malignancy, HIV infection, and a constellation of signs and symptoms, underscoring its importance for diagnosis, monitoring, and integrated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1701
retrieved_docs.content: Mitomycin and mercaptopurine are both contraindicated in diseases of white blood cells, meaning that their use should be avoided in patients who have conditions affecting white blood cell production or function. This precaution reflects the potential for these agents to exacerbate hematologic abnormalities and underscores the importance of evaluating white blood cell status before initiating therapy with either drug.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: Diseases of white blood cells represent a group of hematologic disorders in which the normal function or number of leukocytes is impaired. The chemotherapeutic agents mitomycin, karmustin, and merkaptopurin are contraindicated for use in patients with these diseases, meaning that their administration could exacerbate the underlying condition or cause harmful effects. Conversely, meropenem anhydrous, a broad‑spectrum β‑lactam antibiotic, may be employed as a therapeutic option to treat infections that arise in the context of diseases of white blood cells, providing coverage against a wide range of bacterial pathogens that such immunocompromised patients are prone to develop. Careful selection of medications—avoiding the contraindicated agents while considering appropriate antimicrobial therapy such as meropenem anhydrous—is essential for safe and effective management of patients with white‑blood‑cell disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1802
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this hemoglobin disorder commonly manifests as نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating reduced oxygen levels in the blood.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1152
retrieved_docs.content: Acute anemia resulting from blood loss and anemia resulting from blood loss are both classified as members of the “Bleeding terms (excluding laboratory terms) (SMQ)” category, indicating that they belong to the same standardized group of bleeding‑related concepts. Hemorrhagic infiltration is clinically associated with anemia resulting from blood loss, reflecting that the presence of blood‑filled tissue infiltration often co‑occurs with or contributes to the development of anemia due to loss of blood. This interrelationship highlights how specific bleeding manifestations and their resulting anemic states are organized within a common terminology framework and are linked in clinical presentation.
